[National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine](/wiki/National_Academies_of_Sciences,_Engineering,_and_Medicine "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine")

(**NAS**) is an American nonprofit, [non-governmental organization](/wiki/NGO "NGO"). NAS is part of the [National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine](/wiki/National_Academies_of_Sciences,_Engineering,_and_Medicine "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine"), along with the [National Academy of Engineering](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Engineering "National Academy of Engineering") (NAE) and the [National Academy of Medicine](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Medicine "National Academy of Medicine") (NAM).

The **National Academy of Sciences** (**NAS**) is an American nonprofit, [non-governmental organization](/wiki/NGO "NGO"). NAS is part of the [National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine](/wiki/National_Academies_of_Sciences,_Engineering,_and_Medicine "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine"), along with the [National Academy of Engineering](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Engineering "National Academy of Engineering") (NAE) and the [National Academy of Medicine](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Medicine "National Academy of Medicine") (NAM).

As a [national academy](/wiki/National_academy "National academy"), new members of the organization are elected annually by current members, based on their distinguished and continuing achievements in original research. Election to the National Academy is one of the highest honors in the scientific field in the United States. [Members of the National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Member_of_the_National_Academy_of_Sciences "Member of the National Academy of Sciences") serve *[pro bono](/wiki/Pro_bono "Pro bono")* as "advisers to the nation" on science, engineering, and medicine. The group holds a [congressional charter](/wiki/Congressional_charter "Congressional charter") under [Title 36 of the United States Code](/wiki/Title_36_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 36 of the United States Code").

Congress legislated and President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") signed an [Act of Congress](/wiki/Act_of_Congress "Act of Congress") (1863) establishing the National Academy of Sciences as an independent, trusted nongovernmental institution, created for the purpose of "providing independent, objective advice to the nation on matters related to science and technology [and] to provide scientific advice to the government 'whenever called upon' by any government department."[[2]](#cite_note-2) This objective gave the academy the purpose of enriching and providing resources to any part of the federal government—rather than serving a single branch or executive agency, in contrast to the Library of Congress or many entities that report to the President. The goal was somewhat unusual at the time, and also different than other knowledge-based entities serving a branch of government, such as the Library of Congress. The academy receives no compensation from the government for its services.[[3]](#cite_note-NASmission-3)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Sciences&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Sciences&action=edit), the National Academy of Sciences includes 2,687 [NAS members](/wiki/Member_of_the_National_Academy_of_Sciences "Member of the National Academy of Sciences") and 531 [international members](/wiki/Foreign_Associate_of_the_National_Academy_of_Sciences "Foreign Associate of the National Academy of Sciences").[[4]](#cite_note-aboutnas-4) It employed about 1,100 staff in 2005.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-nasoverview-6) Some 190 members have won a Nobel Prize.[[4]](#cite_note-aboutnas-4) By its own admission in 1989, the addition of women to the academy "continues at a dismal trickle"; at that time there were 1,516 male members and 57 female members.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The National Academy of Sciences is one of the 135 member organizations of the [International Science Council](/wiki/International_Science_Council "International Science Council") (ISC). Although there is no formal relationship with state and local academies of science, there often is informal dialogue. The National Academy is governed by a 17-member Council, made up of five officers (president, vice president, home secretary, international secretary, and treasurer) and 12 Councilors, all of whom are elected from among the academy membership.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Agencies of the United States government fund about 85 percent of the academy's activities. Further funding comes from state governments, private foundations, and industrial organizations.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The council has the ability, ad hoc, to delegate certain tasks to committees. For example, the Committee on Animal Nutrition has produced a series of *Nutrient requirements of domestic animals* reports since at least 1944, each one being initiated by a different subcommittee of experts in the field, for example, on [dairy cattle](/wiki/Dairy_cattle "Dairy cattle").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The National Academy of Sciences meets annually in Washington, D.C., which is documented in the *[Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Proceedings_of_the_National_Academy_of_Sciences "Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences")* (PNAS), its scholarly journal. The [National Academies Press](/wiki/National_Academies_Press "National Academies Press") is the publisher for the National Academies and makes more than 5,000 publications freely available on its website.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

From 2004 to 2017, the National Academy of Sciences administered the [Marian Koshland Science Museum](/wiki/Marian_Koshland_Science_Museum "Marian Koshland Science Museum") to provide public exhibits and programming related to its policy work. The museum's exhibits focused on [climate change](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming") and [infectious disease](/wiki/Infectious_disease "Infectious disease"). In 2017, the museum closed and made way for a new science outreach program called LabX.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Sciences&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Keck_Center_of_the_National_Academies.JPG/250px-Keck_Center_of_the_National_Academies.JPG)](/wiki/File:Keck_Center_of_the_National_Academies.JPG)

The Keck Center of the National Academies in Washington, D.C., one of several facilities where the National Academy of Sciences maintains offices

The Act of Incorporation, signed by President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") on March 3, 1863, created the National Academy of Sciences and named 50 charter members. Many of the original [NAS members](/wiki/Member_of_the_National_Academy_of_Sciences "Member of the National Academy of Sciences") came from the so-called "[Scientific Lazzaroni](/wiki/Scientific_Lazzaroni "Scientific Lazzaroni")", an informal network of mostly physical scientists working in the vicinity of [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts") (c. 1850).[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In 1863, the organizers enlisted the support of [Alexander Dallas Bache](/wiki/Alexander_Dallas_Bache "Alexander Dallas Bache"), and also [Charles Henry Davis](/wiki/Charles_Henry_Davis "Charles Henry Davis"), a professional [astronomer](/wiki/Astronomer "Astronomer") who had been recently recalled from the Navy to [Washington](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") to head the [Bureau of Navigation](/wiki/Bureau_of_Navigation_(United_States_Navy) "Bureau of Navigation (United States Navy)"). They also elicited support from Swiss-American geologist [Louis Agassiz](/wiki/Louis_Agassiz "Louis Agassiz") and American mathematician [Peirce](/wiki/Benjamin_Peirce "Benjamin Peirce"), who together planned the steps whereby the National Academy of Sciences was to be established. Senator [Henry Wilson](/wiki/Henry_Wilson "Henry Wilson") of Massachusetts was to name Agassiz to the Board of Regents of the [Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Agassiz was to come to Washington, D.C., at the government's expense to plan the organization with the others. This bypassed [Joseph Henry](/wiki/Joseph_Henry "Joseph Henry"), who was reluctant to have a bill for such an academy presented to [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"). This was in the belief that such a resolution would be "opposed as something at variance with our democratic institutions". Nevertheless, Henry soon became the second President of NAS. Agassiz, Davis, Peirce, [Benjamin Gould](/wiki/Benjamin_Gould "Benjamin Gould") and Senator Wilson met at Bache's house and "hurriedly wrote the bill incorporating the Academy, including in it the name of fifty incorporators".[[15]](#cite_note-Miller-15)

During the last hours of the session, when the Senate was immersed in the rush of last-minute business before its adjournment, Senator Wilson introduced the bill. Without examining it or debating its provisions, both the Senate and House approved it, and President Lincoln signed it.[[15]](#cite_note-Miller-15)

Although hailed as a great step forward in government recognition of the role of science in American society, at the time, the National Academy of Sciences created enormous ill feelings among scientists,[[15]](#cite_note-Miller-15) whether or not they were named as incorporators.

The act states:

> [T]he Academy shall, whenever called upon by any department of the Government, investigate, examine, experiment, and report upon any subject of science or art, the actual expense of such investigations, examinations, experiments, and reports to be paid from appropriations which may be made for the purpose, but the Academy shall receive no compensation whatever for any services to the Government of the United States.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The National Academies did not solve the problems facing a nation in [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") as the Lazzaroni had hoped, nor did it centralize American scientific efforts.[[15]](#cite_note-Miller-15) However, election to the National Academy did come to be considered "the pinnacle of scientific achievement for Americans" until the establishment of the Nobel Prize at the end of the 19th century.[[17]](#cite_note-Stankus_p.-17): 30

In 1870, the congressional charter was amended to remove the limitation on the number of members.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In 2013, astrophysicist [Neil deGrasse Tyson](/wiki/Neil_deGrasse_Tyson "Neil deGrasse Tyson") was asked to write a speech for the 150th anniversary of the [Gettysburg Address](/wiki/Gettysburg_Address#Legacy "Gettysburg Address") in which he made the point that one of Lincoln's greatest legacies was establishing NAS in that same year, which had the long-term effect of "setting our Nation on a course of scientifically enlightened governance, without which we all may perish from this Earth".[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Membership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Sciences&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Membership")]

The academy currently (as of late 2024) has 6,892 members, including international ones, both past and present. 3218 of them are living. Existing members elect new members for life. Up to 120 members are elected annually, while up to 30 foreign citizens may be elected as international members annually. The election process begins with a formal nomination, followed by a vetting period, and culminates in a final ballot at the academy's annual meeting in April each year. Members are affiliated with a specific scientific field in one of six so-called "classes", which include: Physical and Mathematical Sciences; Biological Sciences; Engineering and Applied Sciences; Biomedical Sciences; Behavioral and [Social Sciences](/wiki/Social_science "Social science"); and Applied Biological, Agricultural, and Environmental Sciences.[[6]](#cite_note-nasoverview-6)

Over the entire history of the NAS, [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") is associated with the most members (331) overall, while the [University of California at Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley") is associated with the most members (255) without including the medical school. Examples of top schools, UC Berkeley/MIT/Princeton/Caltech do not have medical schools, while Harvard/Stanford do. The top ten institutions, two of which are from the [University of California System](/wiki/University_of_California "University of California") and another four of which are in the [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League"), account for nearly 28% of all members ever elected. Those ten are also precisely the only institutions in the entire history of the NAS to have had 100 or more members overall.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On the list for living members, only 14 institutions have 50 or more members overall, including the medical school (where it applies). They represent 32% of all living members of the NAS.

## Facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Sciences&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Facilities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Beckman_Conference_Center%2C_National_Academies_%28USA%29.JPG/250px-Beckman_Conference_Center%2C_National_Academies_%28USA%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Beckman_Conference_Center,_National_Academies_(USA).JPG)

The National Academies' Beckman Conference Center in [Irvine, California](/wiki/Irvine,_California "Irvine, California")

The National Academy of Sciences maintains multiple buildings around the United States. The [National Academy of Sciences Building](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences_Building "National Academy of Sciences Building") is located at 2101 [Constitution Avenue](/wiki/Constitution_Avenue "Constitution Avenue"), in northwest [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."); it sits on the [National Mall](/wiki/National_Mall "National Mall"), adjacent to the [Marriner S. Eccles Federal Reserve Board Building](/wiki/Marriner_S._Eccles_Federal_Reserve_Board_Building "Marriner S. Eccles Federal Reserve Board Building") and in front of the [headquarters](/wiki/Harry_S_Truman_Building "Harry S Truman Building") of the [U.S. State Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State"). The building has a [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") architectural style and was built by architect [Bertram Grosvenor Goodhue](/wiki/Bertram_Grosvenor_Goodhue "Bertram Grosvenor Goodhue"). The building was dedicated in 1924[[57]](#cite_note-57) and is listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"). Goodhue engaged a team of artists and architectural sculptors, including [Albert Herter](/wiki/Albert_Herter "Albert Herter"), [Lee Lawrie](/wiki/Lee_Lawrie "Lee Lawrie"), and [Hildreth Meière](/wiki/Hildreth_Mei%C3%A8re "Hildreth Meière") to design interior embellishments celebrating the history and significance of science.[[58]](#cite_note-ConstitutionAve-58) The building is used for lectures, symposia, exhibitions, and concerts, in addition to annual meetings of the NAS, NAE, and NAM. Cultural Programs of the National Academy of Sciences hosts exhibitions exploring intersections of art, science, and culture such as [Mathemalchemy](/wiki/Mathemalchemy "Mathemalchemy").[[59]](#cite_note-59) The 2012 Presidential Award for Math and Science Teaching ceremony was held here on March 5, 2014. Approximately 150 staff members work at the NAS Building. In June 2012, it reopened to visitors after a major two-year restoration project, which restored and improved the building's historic spaces, increased accessibility, and brought the building's aging infrastructure and facilities up to date.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

More than 1,000 National Academies staff members work at The Keck Center of the National Academies at 500 Fifth Street in northwest Washington, D.C. The Keck Center provides meeting space and houses the National Academies Press Bookstore.[[61]](#cite_note-NASbuildings-61) The [Marian Koshland Science Museum](/wiki/Marian_Koshland_Science_Museum "Marian Koshland Science Museum") of the National Academy of Sciences – formerly located at 525 E St., N.W. – hosted visits from the public, school field trips, and permanent science exhibits.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

NAS also maintains conference centers in California and Massachusetts.[[61]](#cite_note-NASbuildings-61) The Arnold and Mabel Beckman Center is located on 100 Academy Drive in [Irvine, California](/wiki/Irvine,_California "Irvine, California"), near the campus of the [University of California, Irvine](/wiki/University_of_California,_Irvine "University of California, Irvine"); it offers a conference center and houses several NAS programs. The J. Erik Jonsson Conference Center, located at 314 Quissett Avenue in [Woods Hole, Massachusetts](/wiki/Woods_Hole,_Massachusetts "Woods Hole, Massachusetts"), is an NAS conference facility.[[63]](#cite_note-JonssonCenter-63)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Presidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Sciences&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Presidents")]

The president is the head of the academy, elected by a majority vote of the membership to serve in this position for a term to be determined by the governing Council, not to exceed six years, and may be re-elected for a second term. Terms usually start on July 1 and end on June 30. The academy has had 22 presidents since its foundation.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The current president is [geophysicist](/wiki/Geophysics "Geophysics") [Marcia K. McNutt](/wiki/Marcia_McNutt "Marcia McNutt"), the first woman to hold this position. Her term expires on June 30, 2026.[[65]](#cite_note-naspresidents-65)

## Joint Declaration on Global Warming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Sciences&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Joint Declaration on Global Warming")]

In 2005, the national science academies of the [G8](/wiki/G8 "G8") forum (including the National Academy of Sciences) and science academies of Brazil, China, and India (three of the largest emitters of [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") in the developing world) signed a statement on the global response to [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"). The statement stresses that the scientific understanding of climate change has become sufficiently clear to justify nations taking prompt action.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

On May 7, 2010, a letter signed by 255 Academy members was published in [*Science* magazine](/wiki/Science_(journal) "Science (journal)"), decrying "political assaults" against climate change scientists.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76) This was in response to a [civil investigative demand](/wiki/Civil_investigative_demand "Civil investigative demand") on the [University of Virginia](/wiki/University_of_Virginia "University of Virginia") (UVA) by Virginia Attorney General [Ken Cuccinelli](/wiki/Ken_Cuccinelli "Ken Cuccinelli"), seeking a broad range of documents from [Michael E. Mann](/wiki/Michael_E._Mann "Michael E. Mann"), a former UVA professor from 1999 to 2005.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-wp592010-78) Mann, who currently works at the [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania"), is a climate change researcher, and Cuccinelli alleges that Mann may have defrauded Virginia taxpayers in the course of his environmental research. Investigations had cleared Mann of charges that he falsified or suppressed data.[[79]](#cite_note-PSU_Findings-79)

## Notable appointments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Academy_of_Sciences&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Notable appointments")]

* 1873, [Edward C. Pickering](/wiki/Edward_C._Pickering "Edward C. Pickering") (1846–1919) was the youngest scientist elected[[80]](#cite_note-FAQ-80)
* 1924, [Florence R. Sabin](/wiki/Florence_R._Sabin "Florence R. Sabin") (1871–1953) was the first lifetime woman member to be elected[[80]](#cite_note-FAQ-80)
* 1965, [David Blackwell](/wiki/David_Blackwell "David Blackwell") (1919–2010) was the first African-American elected[[80]](#cite_note-FAQ-80)
* 2013, [Ben Barres](/wiki/Ben_Barres "Ben Barres") (1954–2017) was the first openly [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") scientist elected[[81]](#cite_note-81)
* 2024, [Peter Ungar](/wiki/Peter_Ungar "Peter Ungar") (1963–present) was the first [Arkansan](/wiki/Arkansan "Arkansan") elected[[82]](#cite_note-82)
